chunks.title: Wrap Preparation — All Varieties
chunks.content: PESTO PANEER WRAP: Tortilla wrap toast and reheat. Add 1x 85ml SCOOP + 1x scoop. Add 1x tong. Add 8-10 slices pesto paneer. Add 1x tong. Wrap in parchment paper. Roll, position open end facing down. Cut diagonally. Serve with chips. WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. Includes 1x sachet. MUSHROOM & CHEESE WRAP: Tortilla wrap toast and reheat. Add 1x 85ml SCOOP + 1x scoop. Add 1x tong. Add 1x tong mushroom. Wrap in parchment paper. Cut diagonally. Serve with chips. WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. Includes 1x sachet and melted butter brush. PANEER TIKKA WRAP: Add 1 scoop + 1 tong. Wrap, cut and serve with chips. Apply 15 zig-zag makhani mayo. WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. WITH BLACK TRAY for tortilla wrap toast reheat. TANDOORI CHICKEN WRAP: Add 1 scoop + 1 tong. Apply 15 lines makhani mayo. Wrap, cut and serve with chips. Position open end facing down. WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. CORN & CHEESE WRAP (JAIN): Toast tortilla and reheat. Add 1x 85ml SCOOP. Add 10 slices + 1 slice. Add 2 halves. Add 1x tong. Wrap in parchment paper. Cut diagonally. Add 10x lines. Serve with chips. WITHOUT BLACK TRAY.

chunks.title: Pizza Preparation — All Varieties
chunks.content: MUSHROOM & ONION PIZZA: Sautée mushroom and onion. Scrape the pizza off and spread the mix on the pizza base. Mix it well. Serve WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. MARGHERITA PIZZA (VEG): Use 1x thawed base. Scrape off parchment. Add extra virgin olive oil on the sides. Pizza heat. Cut into 6 slices. Garnish with 6 basil leaves. Serve WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. TANDOORI CHICKEN PIZZA (NON-VEG): Use 1x thawed base. Scrape off parchment. Add extra virgin olive oil on the sides. Pizza heat. Add tandoori chicken. Add jalapenos and onion slices. Cut into 6 slices. Serve WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. MEXICAN PANEER PIZZA (VEG): Use 1x thawed base. Scrape off parchment. Add Mexican paneer. Add bell pepper, olives, red paprika. Cut into 6 slices. Serve WITHOUT BLACK TRAY. VEG BONANZA PIZZA: Use 1x thawed base. Add extra virgin olive oil on the sides. Pizza heat. Add red paprika, jalapenos, olives, onion slices, bell pepper. Cut into 6 slices. Serve WITHOUT BLACK TRAY.

qa_pairs.instruction: How do I make a margherita pizza?

qa_pairs.instruction: How do I make a tandoori chicken pizza?

chunks.title: Hot Beverage Specials Preparation
chunks.content: SEA SALT MOCHA: Espresso rounds: 3R/4L (Regular/Large). Add 15ml pump sea salt. Add chocolate sauce. Steamed milk: 160ml R / 260ml L. Latte consistency. Garnish: sea salt on top. Shakes available: 3R 3L. HAZELNUT CAPPUCCINO: Espresso rounds: 2R 3L. Add hazelnut syrup (7.5ml). Steamed milk at 50% foam (cappuccino consistency). Garnish with roasted hazelnut. FRENCH VANILLA LATTE: Espresso rounds: 2R 3L. Add vanilla syrup (7.5ml). Steamed milk in latte consistency. TOFFEE NUT LATTE: Espresso rounds: 2R 3L (or 2.5R 2.5L). Add toffee nut sauce (10ml). Steamed milk: 160ml R / 260ml L in latte consistency. Garnish with 1 scoop butter crumbs. Rounds: 1R 1L / 1R 1M.

chunks.title: Serving Standards — Black Tray and Presentation Rules
chunks.content: TWC items have specific serving tray rules. Items served WITH BLACK TRAY: all bagels, focaccia sandwiches, all salads, pesto paneer quesadilla, non-veg sandwiches (tandoori chicken, spicy chicken, peppery chicken), hummus and pita platter. Items served WITHOUT BLACK TRAY: all veg sandwiches, chilli cheese toast, paneer and corn toast, baked chilli cheese toast, all toasties, all wraps, mushroom & cheese quesadilla, all pizzas, corn & cheese wrap. CUT RULES: Most veg sandwiches are DO NOT CUT. Bagels for dine-in are cut vertically; for TA/HD (takeaway/home delivery) DO NOT CUT. Non-veg sandwiches are assembled and cut down the middle. All pizzas are cut into 6 slices. Wraps are cut diagonally. All sandwiches served with chips.

qa_pairs.instruction: Which items are served without a black tray?
qa_pairs.output: Items served WITHOUT black tray: all veg sandwiches, chilli cheese toast, paneer and corn toast, baked chilli cheese toast, all toasties, all wraps, mushroom & cheese quesadilla, and all pizzas.

qa_pairs.instruction: Which sandwiches should not be cut?
qa_pairs.output: Most veg sandwiches are DO NOT CUT (thecha aloo, creamy corn, loaded Bombay masala). Bagels for takeaway/home delivery are also DO NOT CUT. Non-veg sandwiches ARE cut down the middle.

chunks.title: Beverage Build Pattern — Universal Hot Drink Structure
chunks.content: UNIVERSAL HOT BEVERAGE BUILD ORDER AT TWC: Almost all hot specialty beverages at TWC follow the same fundamental build sequence: Step 1: FLAVOUR/SYRUP — Add the flavour component first into the cup. This could be a syrup pump (hazelnut 7.5ml, vanilla 7.5ml, orange dry pump), a sauce (chocolate sauce, toffee nut sauce 10ml), or a seasoning (sea salt 15ml pump). Step 2: ESPRESSO — Pull the required espresso rounds into the cup on top of the flavour. Round counts vary by drink and size: Regular drinks typically use 1-2 rounds, Large drinks use 2-3 rounds. Examples: Sea Salt Mocha is 3R/4L, Hazelnut Cappuccino is 2R/3L, Toffee Nut Latte is 2R/3L. Step 3: MILK — Steam milk to the correct consistency and pour. Lattes use steamed milk in latte consistency (thin microfoam). Cappuccinos use steamed milk with 50% foam (thicker, airier). Milk volumes: Regular ~160ml, Large ~260ml. Step 4: GARNISH — Add the finishing touch on top. Examples: sea salt sprinkle, orange zest, roasted hazelnuts, butter crumbs (1 scoop for Toffee Nut). PATTERN INSIGHT: The Flavour→Espresso→Milk→Garnish sequence is consistent across Sea Salt Mocha, Orange Chocolate Mocha, Hazelnut Cappuccino, French Vanilla Latte, and Toffee Nut Latte. The only variables that change between drinks are: (a) which flavour/syrup, (b) how many espresso rounds, (c) latte vs cappuccino milk consistency, and (d) which garnish. LATTE vs CAPPUCCINO at TWC: The key difference is milk texture. A latte uses steamed milk with thin microfoam (latte consistency). A cappuccino uses steamed milk with 50% foam — more airy and frothy. Both start with syrup + espresso, but the milk pour technique differs.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the general build order for hot beverages at TWC?
qa_pairs.output:
All hot specialty beverages at TWC follow the same build order:
1. **Flavour/Syrup** — Add syrup, sauce or seasoning first into the cup
2. **Espresso** — Pull the required rounds on top
3. **Milk** — Steam and pour to the correct consistency (latte or cappuccino)
4. **Garnish** — Add the finishing touch on top

This Flavour→Espresso→Milk→Garnish pattern applies to Sea Salt Mocha, Hazelnut Cappuccino, Toffee Nut Latte, French Vanilla Latte, and Orange Chocolate Mocha.

qa_pairs.instruction: How are all beverages made at TWC?
qa_pairs.output:
All hot specialty beverages follow the same 4-step build:
1. **Flavour** — Syrup/sauce goes in first (hazelnut, vanilla, toffee nut, chocolate, sea salt, orange)
2. **Espresso** — Espresso rounds pulled on top (varies by drink and size)
3. **Milk** — Steamed milk poured (latte consistency or 50% foam for cappuccino)
4. **Garnish** — Finishing touch (sea salt, orange zest, hazelnuts, butter crumbs, basil)

The drink name tells you the flavour and milk style. The structure is always the same.

qa_pairs.instruction: What's the difference between a latte and cappuccino at TWC?
qa_pairs.output:
The build order is the same (Flavour→Espresso→Milk→Garnish), but the milk is different:
- **Latte:** Steamed milk with thin microfoam (latte consistency) — smooth, silky
- **Cappuccino:** Steamed milk with 50% foam — more airy and frothy

Example: French Vanilla Latte and Hazelnut Cappuccino both have syrup + espresso, but the Hazelnut Cap gets foamier milk.

qa_pairs.instruction: What's the pattern behind all the hot drinks?
qa_pairs.output:
Every hot specialty drink follows: **Flavour → Espresso → Milk → Garnish**. What changes between drinks is:
- **Which flavour:** hazelnut syrup, vanilla syrup, toffee nut sauce, chocolate sauce, sea salt, orange syrup
- **How many espresso rounds:** typically 1-2 for Regular, 2-3 for Large
- **Milk style:** latte consistency (thin foam) or cappuccino consistency (50% foam)
- **Which garnish:** roasted hazelnuts, sea salt, orange zest, butter crumbs

qa_pairs.instruction: Why does the flavour go first in beverages?
qa_pairs.output: The flavour/syrup goes in first so the espresso extracts directly onto it, which helps integrate the flavour into the drink. When you pour steamed milk on top, it blends everything together. If you added syrup after milk, it would sit on top and not mix evenly.

qa_pairs.instruction: How do espresso rounds differ between regular and large drinks?
qa_pairs.output:
Large drinks get more espresso to maintain flavour intensity with the extra milk:
- **Sea Salt Mocha:** Regular 3R, Large 4L
- **Hazelnut Cappuccino:** Regular 2R, Large 3L
- **Toffee Nut Latte:** Regular 2R, Large 3L
- **Milk volume also scales:** Regular ~160ml, Large ~260ml

The ratio of espresso-to-milk stays roughly consistent across sizes.

qa_pairs.instruction: What do all TWC beverages have in common?
qa_pairs.output:
All hot specialty beverages share:
- Same build sequence: Flavour → Espresso → Milk → Garnish
- Espresso as the base (pulled from dialled-in machine)
- Steamed milk (either latte or cappuccino consistency)
- A unique flavour/syrup and matching garnish that gives each drink its identity

qa_pairs.instruction: Explain the beverage structure to a new barista
qa_pairs.output:
Think of every hot drink as 4 layers built in order:
1. **Flavour** — Your syrup or sauce goes in the cup first (this is what makes it a Hazelnut Cap vs a Toffee Nut Latte)
2. **Espresso** — Pull your rounds on top of the flavour
3. **Milk** — Steam it right (thin foam for lattes, 50% foam for cappuccinos) and pour
4. **Garnish** — The finishing touch that makes it look complete

Once you know this structure, you can make any drink — you just swap out the variables.

chunks.title: Food Preparation Patterns — Common Build Structures
chunks.content: COMMON FOOD PREPARATION PATTERNS AT TWC: Different food categories follow consistent build patterns. SANDWICH PATTERN (Veg & Non-Veg): Toast bread → Spread/sauce on plain side → Add filling → Add cheese → Heat/melt → Wrap → Serve with chips. Veg sandwiches: DO NOT CUT, served WITHOUT black tray. Non-veg sandwiches: CUT diagonally, served WITH black tray. BAGEL PATTERN: Cut bagel in half → Toast → Spread (cream cheese, pesto, or protein) on crown and/or heel → Add toppings on heel → Assemble → Serve WITH black tray. Dine-in: cut vertically. TA/HD: DO NOT CUT. PIZZA PATTERN: Thaw base → Scrape parchment → Add EVOO on sides → Heat → Add toppings → Cut into 6 slices → Serve WITHOUT black tray. All 5 pizzas follow this exact same process. CROISSANT PATTERN: Cut into 2 halves → Spread (hummus, mustard, or butter) on heel → Layer fillings → Close with crown → Serve in paper pocket + tissue. WRAP PATTERN: Lay tortilla flat → Spread sauce → Add filling → Add cheese → Roll tight → Heat → Cut diagonally → Serve. QUESADILLA PATTERN: Lay tortilla → Spread filling on one half → Fold → Heat → Cut into triangles → Serve. TOAST PATTERN: Toast bread → Add filling → Add cheese → Heat/melt → Cut into triangles. SALAD PATTERN: Base greens in bowl → Add protein/veg components → Drizzle dressing → Garnish → Serve WITH black tray.

qa_pairs.instruction: What are the common food preparation patterns at TWC?
qa_pairs.output:
Each food category follows a consistent build pattern:
- **Sandwiches:** Toast → Spread/sauce → Filling → Cheese → Heat → Wrap → Serve with chips
- **Bagels:** Cut → Toast → Spread → Toppings → Assemble → Serve with black tray
- **Pizzas:** Thaw base → EVOO → Heat → Toppings → Cut into 6 slices
- **Croissants:** Cut halves → Spread on heel → Layer fillings → Close → Paper pocket
- **Wraps:** Tortilla → Sauce → Filling → Cheese → Roll → Heat → Cut diagonal
- **Quesadillas:** Tortilla → Filling on half → Fold → Heat → Cut triangles
- **Salads:** Greens → Components → Dressing → Garnish → Black tray

qa_pairs.instruction: How are all sandwiches made?
qa_pairs.output:
All TWC sandwiches follow the same pattern:
1. **Toast** the bread
2. **Spread** sauce on the plain side (thecha, honey mustard, peri peri, etc.)
3. **Add filling** (aloo, corn, chicken, etc.)
4. **Add cheese** and melt
5. **Heat** with parchment paper
6. **Wrap and serve** with chips

Key difference: Veg sandwiches are NOT cut and served WITHOUT black tray. Non-veg are CUT diagonally and served WITH black tray.

qa_pairs.instruction: What's the general process for making any pizza?

4. Heat the base
5. Add the specific toppings
6. Cut into 6 slices
7. Serve WITHOUT black tray

The only thing that changes between pizzas is the toppings.

2. Toast
3. Spread on crown and/or heel (cream cheese, pesto, protein spread)

Dine-in: some are cut vertically. Takeaway/HD: NEVER cut.

Every wrap follows the same build:

2. Spread sauce
3. Add filling (paneer tikka, pesto paneer, chicken, etc.)

The wrap type just determines which filling and sauce go in.

There's a consistent serving rule:

- **Focaccia** (pesto paneer, spiced chicken)
- **Wraps** (4 varieties)
- **Quesadillas** (3 varieties)
- **Pizzas** (5 varieties)
- **Salads** (2 varieties)
- **Toasts** (3 varieties)
- **Pancakes** (2 varieties)
- **Quick Bites** (6 varieties)
- **Smoothie Bowls** (2 varieties)
- **Hummus Platter**
- **Hot Beverage Specials** (5 specialty drinks)

chunks.title: Espresso & Dial-In Pattern — Logic and Troubleshooting Flow
chunks.content: ESPRESSO DIAL-IN LOGIC FLOW: The dial-in process follows a systematic feedback loop: Dose→Tamp→Extract→Taste→Adjust→Repeat. DOSE: Weigh ground coffee to the recipe card standard. TAMP: Apply even, consistent pressure to level the coffee bed. EXTRACT: Pull the shot and observe flow + time. TASTE: Evaluate against sensory benchmarks (visuals: reddish-brown crema, smooth; aroma: dark chocolate, roasted nuts, citrus; taste: balanced sweetness, sourness, bitterness). ADJUST: If shot is too fast (under-extracted: watery, sour) → grind finer. If too slow (over-extracted: bitter, astringent) → grind coarser. Then repeat. KEY INSIGHT: The dial-in is a calibration loop, not a one-time setup. You keep pulling and adjusting until the shot meets all three sensory criteria. It must be done at every shift start and whenever beans change. ESPRESSO ROUND PATTERNS ACROSS DRINKS: Different drinks use different round counts to balance flavour with milk volume. More rounds = stronger coffee presence in larger drinks. Regular drinks: 1-3 rounds. Large drinks: 2-4 rounds. The ratio is designed so the coffee flavour is consistent regardless of drink size.

qa_pairs.instruction: What's the logic behind the dial-in process?
qa_pairs.output:
The dial-in is a **feedback loop**: Dose → Tamp → Extract → Taste → Adjust → Repeat.

The logic:
1. Set your dose to the recipe card standard
2. Tamp evenly
3. Pull a shot and observe flow + timing
4. Taste against 3 benchmarks: visuals (reddish-brown crema), aroma (chocolate/nuts/citrus), taste (balanced sweet/sour/bitter)
5. If it's off — adjust grind (finer for sour/fast, coarser for bitter/slow)
6. Repeat until all 3 benchmarks are met

It's not a one-time setup — it's continuous calibration.

qa_pairs.instruction: Why do we adjust grind size?
qa_pairs.output:
Grind size controls extraction time, which directly affects taste:
- **Too fast (under-extracted):** Water passes through too quickly → sour, watery, thin body → **Fix: grind finer** (slower flow)
- **Too slow (over-extracted):** Water takes too long → bitter, astringent, burnt → **Fix: grind coarser** (faster flow)

The goal is a balanced shot: medium sweetness, medium sourness, medium bitterness.

qa_pairs.instruction: Why do large drinks have more espresso rounds?
qa_pairs.output:
Large drinks have more milk (260ml vs 160ml for regular). If you kept the same espresso rounds, the coffee flavour would be diluted. More rounds maintain the coffee-to-milk ratio so the drink tastes equally strong regardless of size.

Example: Hazelnut Cappuccino uses 2 rounds for Regular but 3 for Large.

qa_pairs.instruction: When should dial-in be done?
qa_pairs.output:
Two mandatory triggers:
1. **Start of every shift** — the machine, grinder, and environment change over time
2. **Whenever coffee beans are changed** — different beans extract differently

It's not optional — it's daily calibration to ensure consistent quality.

qa_pairs.instruction: What are the sensory benchmarks for a good espresso shot?
qa_pairs.output:
Three criteria, all must be met:
- **Visuals:** Reddish-brown crema, no large bubbles, smooth velvety texture
- **Aroma:** Notes of dark chocolate, roasted nuts, and citrus
- **Taste:** Medium sweetness, medium sourness (bright acidity), medium bitterness — balanced

If any of these are off, keep adjusting grind and dose until all three align.

chunks.title: Serving Rules Pattern — Tray, Cutting, and Presentation Logic
chunks.content: SERVING RULES PATTERN ANALYSIS: TWC has consistent logic behind serving rules. BLACK TRAY RULE: Items that are plated, open, or have a more premium/complex presentation use the black tray. Items that are wrapped, closed, or simpler are served without. WITH BLACK TRAY: all bagels, focaccia, salads, non-veg sandwiches, hummus platter, pesto paneer quesadilla. WITHOUT BLACK TRAY: all veg sandwiches, toasts, toasties, wraps, pizzas, regular quesadillas (mushroom & cheese, paneer tikka). CUT RULES PATTERN: Cutting depends on item type and service type. Pizzas: ALWAYS cut into 6 slices. Bagels dine-in: cut vertically. Bagels TA/HD: NEVER cut. Non-veg sandwiches: cut diagonally. Veg sandwiches: NEVER cut. Wraps: cut diagonally. Quesadillas: cut into triangles. CHIPS RULE: ALL sandwiches (veg, non-veg, and all-day breakfast) are served with chips. No other food category gets chips. DINE-IN vs TAKEAWAY DIFFERENCES: Some items change presentation based on service type. Bagels are cut for dine-in but not for TA/HD. Croissants get knife and fork optionally for dine-in. Packaging differs (paper pocket for croissants, wrapping for sandwiches).

qa_pairs.instruction: What's the logic behind the black tray rule?
qa_pairs.output:
The pattern is:
- **Black tray** = items that are plated, open, or have premium presentation (bagels, focaccia, salads, non-veg sandwiches, hummus platter)
- **No black tray** = items that are wrapped, closed, or simpler to serve (veg sandwiches, toasts, wraps, pizzas, toasties)

Broadly: if it's presented open on a plate, it gets the tray. If it's wrapped up, it doesn't.

qa_pairs.instruction: What items get chips?
qa_pairs.output: **Only sandwiches get chips** — this includes all veg sandwiches, non-veg sandwiches, and all-day breakfast sandwiches. No other food category (bagels, wraps, pizzas, etc.) is served with chips.

qa_pairs.instruction: What are the cutting rules at TWC?
qa_pairs.output:
Cutting rules by category:
- **Pizzas:** Always cut into 6 slices
- **Non-veg sandwiches:** Cut diagonally
- **Veg sandwiches:** NEVER cut
- **Bagels dine-in:** Cut vertically
- **Bagels TA/HD:** NEVER cut
- **Wraps:** Cut diagonally
- **Quesadillas:** Cut into triangles
- **Toasts:** Cut into triangles

qa_pairs.instruction: What changes between dine-in and takeaway?
qa_pairs.output:
Main differences:
- **Bagels:** Cut vertically for dine-in, NOT cut for takeaway/home delivery
- **Croissants:** Knife and fork offered for dine-in
- **Packaging:** Dine-in uses trays and plates; takeaway uses wrapping, paper pockets, and boxes

The recipe and ingredients stay the same — only plating/cutting/packaging changes.

qa_pairs.instruction: Which items are never cut?
qa_pairs.output:
Items that should NEVER be cut:
- All veg sandwiches (Thecha Aloo, Creamy Corn, Bombay Masala, Mushroom & Onion)
- Bagels for takeaway/home delivery
- Roasted Tomato & Pesto Bagel (never cut regardless of service type)

qa_pairs.instruction: What's the trend in how food is served?
qa_pairs.output:
Clear trends:
1. **Complexity determines tray:** More elaborate = black tray, simpler = no tray
2. **Only sandwiches get chips** — no exceptions
3. **Cut rules follow the item shape:** Pizzas in 6 slices, sandwiches diagonal, bagels vertical, quesadillas in triangles
4. **Dine-in vs takeaway** mainly affects cutting and packaging, never the recipe
5. **Non-veg gets more treatment** than veg (black tray, cutting) across sandwich categories

chunks.title: Size Scaling Pattern — Regular vs Large Differences
chunks.content: SIZE SCALING PATTERN AT TWC: When drinks come in Regular and Large sizes, here's what changes and what stays the same. WHAT CHANGES: Espresso rounds increase (roughly +1 round for Large). Milk volume increases (Regular ~160ml, Large ~260ml — about 60% more). WHAT STAYS THE SAME: Flavour/syrup type and quantity usually stays the same. Garnish type stays the same. Build order stays the same (Flavour→Espresso→Milk→Garnish). Milk consistency stays the same (latte or cappuccino). EXAMPLES: Sea Salt Mocha: R=3 rounds/160ml milk, L=4 rounds/260ml milk. Hazelnut Cappuccino: R=2 rounds, L=3 rounds. Toffee Nut Latte: R=2 rounds, L=3 rounds. KEY INSIGHT: The scaling philosophy is to maintain the taste profile at any size. More milk needs more espresso to keep the ratio. The flavour syrup doesn't need to scale because its intensity is set by the initial pump amount.

qa_pairs.instruction: What changes between regular and large drinks?
qa_pairs.output:
When sizing up from Regular to Large:
- **Espresso:** +1 round (e.g., 2R→3L)
- **Milk:** ~160ml → ~260ml (about 60% more)

What stays the same:
- Flavour/syrup type and amount
- Garnish
- Build order
- Milk consistency (latte or cappuccino)

The goal is to keep the taste profile consistent regardless of size.

qa_pairs.instruction: Why doesn't the syrup amount change for large drinks?
qa_pairs.output: The syrup/flavour pump is set at a specific intensity that works for both sizes. The extra espresso in the large provides enough flavour strength to carry through the additional milk. Increasing syrup would make the large drink too sweet compared to the regular.

qa_pairs.instruction: How does TWC maintain consistent taste across sizes?
qa_pairs.output:
Through proportional scaling:
- More milk in Large → add more espresso to compensate
- Keep syrup the same → it's already at the right intensity
- Same garnish → the visual and finishing touch stays consistent

This ensures a Regular Sea Salt Mocha tastes the same as a Large one.

modules.title: Home Delivery & Takeaway Process (HD Playbook)
modules.description: Complete Home Delivery & Takeaway process for Third Wave Coffee covering portal activation, order receiving, order preparation, packaging, and the ROAST framework for HD excellence.

chunks.title: HD & Takeaway Process — Overview & Objective
chunks.intent: Explain the purpose and scope of the Home Delivery & Takeaway process at TWC.
chunks.content: Home Delivery & Takeaway Process — Objective: Emphasizing the 'Good to Amazing' journey, this document prioritizes absolutely amazing experiences for home delivery and takeaway customers. Detailed steps address key issues, including missing orders and spillage, enhancing the overall process. The aim is to establish a robust and sustainable home delivery and takeaway process for our at-home customers. Our focus is on delivering excellence and minimizing any potential challenges, ensuring a consistently exceptional Third Wave Coffee experience. The process covers 4 main touchpoints: (1) Introduction to the Brand — portal activation and menu accuracy, (2) Order Receiving — auto-acceptance and KOT flow, (3) Order Preparation — SOP adherence and special instructions, (4) Packaging — sealing, spill prevention, and clear identification.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the Home Delivery process at TWC?
qa_pairs.output: TWC's Home Delivery & Takeaway process focuses on the 'Good to Amazing' journey with 4 touchpoints: (1) Introduction to the Brand — portal activation and accurate menu display, (2) Order Receiving — auto-acceptance and KOT printing, (3) Order Preparation — following SOPs and special instructions, (4) Packaging — heat sealing, spill prevention, and labeling.

qa_pairs.instruction: What are the 4 touchpoints of the HD process?
qa_pairs.output:
1. Introduction to the Brand — turning on portals, ensuring accurate product availability on Zomato/Swiggy/App
2. Order Receiving — auto-accepting orders, KOT printing
3. Order Preparation — following SOPs, handling special instructions
4. Packaging — heat sealing cups, spill check, writing product names on cups

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the objective of the HD process?
qa_pairs.output: To establish a robust and sustainable home delivery and takeaway process that delivers an 'absolutely amazing' experience for at-home customers, addresses issues like missing orders and spillage, and ensures a consistently exceptional TWC experience.

qa_pairs.instruction: What does HD mean at TWC?
qa_pairs.output: HD stands for Home Delivery. It refers to the complete process of fulfilling delivery orders from TWC cafés through platforms like Zomato and Swiggy, covering portal activation, order receiving, preparation, and packaging.

chunks.title: HD Smallwares & Equipment
chunks.intent: List all required smallwares and equipment for Home Delivery and Takeaway.
chunks.content: HD Smallwares and Equipment required: Takeaway cup (Hot), Hot Lids, TWC branded aluminium foil, Small Carry bag, Single cup holder, Big Carry bag, Brown Tape, Tamper proof sticker, Cold lids, Takeaway cup (Cold), Double cup holder, Consumables. Cup Sealer Guide: Don't overfill the cup as it may lead to spillage and ineffective sealing. DO leave a 5mm gap from the rim of the cup. Cup Holder Preparation: Fold the long side inwards and short side outwards. Fold the triangles outwards on all four sides. Tuck in the triangles in the groove. Unfold the holder along the seam to make a rectangle.

qa_pairs.instruction: What equipment is needed for HD orders?
qa_pairs.output: Takeaway cups (hot and cold), hot lids, cold lids, TWC branded aluminium foil, small and big carry bags, single and double cup holders, brown tape, tamper proof stickers, and consumables.

qa_pairs.instruction: How do I prepare a cup holder for HD?
qa_pairs.output: Fold the long side inwards and short side outwards. Fold the triangles outwards on all four sides. Tuck in the triangles in the groove. Unfold the holder along the seam to make a rectangle.

qa_pairs.instruction: How much gap should I leave when filling a takeaway cup?
qa_pairs.output: Leave a 5mm gap from the rim of the cup. Don't overfill as it causes spillage and ineffective sealing.

chunks.title: HD Touchpoint 1 — Introduction to the Brand (Portal Activation)
chunks.intent: Explain the first HD touchpoint: portal activation and menu accuracy.
chunks.content: Touchpoint 1 — Introduction to the Brand. Objective: Create a positive first impression and enhance overall brand experience by ensuring cafés are shown online on Zomato, Swiggy, and for app pick-up on time with accurate product availability. This develops trust between the brand and customers. Having the café available to order from with accurate products ensures customers develop trust for the brand. Mitigates negative reviews by ensuring product availability is accurate. Process: Step 1 — Turn on HD portals on Urban Piper. Open Urban Piper on Chrome, click 'Session Settings', ensure Zomato, Swiggy and UrbanPiper are active, click 'Platforms'. Step 2 — Turn off out-of-stock products on Urban Piper and LS. On Urban Piper: Select 'Stock Control', look for unavailable products, turn them off. On LS (for App Pick-up): Click 'New Transaction' under 'In-Store Dine-in', click 'Food Lock', click 'Actions' then 'Lock', choose unavailable products. What if we miss this step? Customer frustration, lost sales, diminished trust, customer attrition, negative reviews, and revenue decline.

qa_pairs.instruction: How do I activate HD portals?
qa_pairs.output: Open Urban Piper on Chrome → click 'Session Settings' → ensure Zomato, Swiggy and UrbanPiper are active → click 'Platforms'. Then turn off any out-of-stock products under 'Stock Control'.

qa_pairs.instruction: How do I turn off out-of-stock items for delivery?
qa_pairs.output: On Urban Piper: Select 'Stock Control' and turn off unavailable products. On LS (for App Pick-up): Click 'New Transaction' under 'In-Store Dine-in' → 'Food Lock' → 'Actions' → 'Lock' → choose unavailable products.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is Touchpoint 1 of the HD process?
qa_pairs.output: Introduction to the Brand — ensuring cafés are live on Zomato, Swiggy, and app pick-up with accurate product availability. Process includes activating portals on Urban Piper and turning off out-of-stock items.

qa_pairs.instruction: What happens if we don't activate HD portals?
qa_pairs.output: Customer frustration, lost sales to competitors, diminished trust, customer attrition, negative online reviews, and overall revenue decline.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is Urban Piper used for?
qa_pairs.output: Urban Piper is the platform used to manage HD portal activation for Zomato, Swiggy, and app pick-up orders. It handles session settings, platform activation, and stock control for delivery menus.

chunks.title: HD Touchpoint 2 — Order Receiving (Auto-Accept)
chunks.intent: Explain the second HD touchpoint: auto-accepting orders.
chunks.content: Touchpoint 2 — Order Receiving. Objective: Auto-accepting online orders ensures a seamless and quick process, enhancing the overall experience without any cancellations. This improves operational efficiency, order accuracy, consistency and reliability, and increases sales and loyalty. Process: The HD order is received and auto-accepted. It shows as 'KOT Printed' on LS and 'Acknowledged' on Urban Piper (Satellite). What if we miss this step? Customer dissatisfaction, lost sales, negative reviews, diminished trust, and operational challenges from handling order cancellations.

qa_pairs.instruction: How are HD orders received?
qa_pairs.output: HD orders are auto-accepted. They show as 'KOT Printed' on LS and 'Acknowledged' on Urban Piper (Satellite). No manual acceptance needed.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is Touchpoint 2 of the HD process?
qa_pairs.output: Order Receiving — orders are auto-accepted, showing as 'KOT Printed' on LS and 'Acknowledged' on Urban Piper. This ensures seamless processing without cancellations.

qa_pairs.instruction: What happens if HD orders are not auto-accepted?
qa_pairs.output: Customer dissatisfaction, lost sales to competitors, negative reviews, diminished trust, and operational challenges from handling cancellations.

chunks.title: HD Touchpoint 3 — Order Preparation
chunks.intent: Explain the third HD touchpoint: preparing orders correctly.
chunks.content: Touchpoint 3 — Order Preparation. Objective: Following Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) ensures consistent preparation, maintaining quality for a uniform customer experience. Adhering to special instructions caters to individual preferences. A systematic approach streamlines the preparation process, promoting efficiency and reducing errors. Process: Immediately after the order is auto-accepted, KOTs for products are printed at respective stations, along with a 'Master KOT' at the POS Printer. Points to observe on a KOT: Order Type, Order ID, Special Instructions, Total Items. Cup Filling Guide: Don't overfill the cup — leave a 5mm gap from the rim for effective sealing. Prepare beverages as per SOP in a takeaway cup. What if we miss this step? Inconsistent quality, customer discontent, customer loss, negative reviews and reputation damage, operational inefficiencies.

qa_pairs.instruction: How are HD orders prepared?
qa_pairs.output: After auto-acceptance, KOTs print at respective stations plus a Master KOT at POS. Check each KOT for: Order Type, Order ID, Special Instructions, Total Items. Prepare beverages per SOP in takeaway cups. Leave a 5mm gap from the rim for sealing.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the Master KOT?
qa_pairs.output: The Master KOT is printed at the POS Printer when an HD order is auto-accepted. It contains the full order details alongside individual station KOTs that print at respective stations (bar, food, etc.).

qa_pairs.instruction: What should I check on a KOT for HD orders?
qa_pairs.output: Check: Order Type (HD/Takeaway), Order ID, Special Instructions from the customer, and Total Items in the order.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is Touchpoint 3 of the HD process?
qa_pairs.output: Order Preparation — following SOPs to prepare orders consistently, adhering to special instructions, using takeaway cups with a 5mm gap from rim, and checking KOTs for all order details.

chunks.title: HD Touchpoint 4 — Packaging
chunks.intent: Explain the fourth HD touchpoint: proper packaging for delivery.
chunks.content: Touchpoint 4 — Packaging. Objective: Heat sealing cups preserves freshness, ensuring customers receive orders in the best possible condition. Spill check and heat-sealing minimize spill risk during transportation. Writing special instructions or product name on the cup provides clarity, ensuring correct orders. Process: Heat seal all cups. Conduct a spill check. Write the product name or special instructions on the cup. Use tamper-proof stickers. Package in appropriate carry bags with cup holders. Impact: Positive first impressions, consistent satisfaction, enhanced brand reputation, operational efficiency. What if we miss this step? Negative first impressions, lost business, diminished trust, and reputation damage.

qa_pairs.instruction: How should HD orders be packaged?
qa_pairs.output: Heat seal all cups → conduct a spill check → write product name or special instructions on the cup → apply tamper-proof stickers → package in appropriate carry bags with cup holders.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is Touchpoint 4 of the HD process?
qa_pairs.output: Packaging — heat sealing cups for freshness, conducting spill checks, writing product names on cups, using tamper-proof stickers, and packaging in carry bags with holders.

qa_pairs.instruction: Why is heat sealing important for HD?
qa_pairs.output: Heat sealing preserves freshness, minimizes spill risk during transportation, and ensures customers receive orders in the best possible condition.

qa_pairs.instruction: What should be written on HD cups?
qa_pairs.output: Write the product name or any special instructions on the cup to ensure the customer receives the correct order.

chunks.title: ROAST — Home Delivery Excellence Framework
chunks.intent: Explain the ROAST acronym framework used in the HD playbook.
chunks.content: ROAST is the Home Delivery excellence framework at Third Wave Coffee. It stands for: R — Readiness (ensure portals are live, stock is accurate, and equipment is ready before orders come in), O — Order Accuracy (auto-accept orders, verify KOTs, follow special instructions precisely), A — Adherence to SOP (prepare every item per standard operating procedure, no shortcuts), S — Sealing & Packaging (heat seal cups, spill check, label clearly, use tamper-proof stickers), T — Timeliness (complete preparation and packaging quickly to meet delivery partner pickup times). ROAST captures the five pillars of the HD process touchpoints in a single framework. Following ROAST ensures every home delivery order meets TWC's quality standards from portal activation to rider handoff.

qa_pairs.instruction: What does ROAST stand for?
qa_pairs.output: ROAST is the Home Delivery excellence framework: R — Readiness (portals live, stock accurate, equipment ready), O — Order Accuracy (auto-accept, verify KOTs, follow special instructions), A — Adherence to SOP (prepare per standard, no shortcuts), S — Sealing & Packaging (heat seal, spill check, label, tamper-proof stickers), T — Timeliness (complete prep and packaging for rider pickup).

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the ROAST framework?
qa_pairs.output: ROAST is TWC's Home Delivery excellence framework with 5 pillars: Readiness, Order Accuracy, Adherence to SOP, Sealing & Packaging, and Timeliness. It captures the complete HD process from portal activation to rider handoff.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the R in ROAST?
qa_pairs.output: R stands for Readiness — ensure portals are live on Zomato/Swiggy, stock is accurate with out-of-stock items turned off, and all equipment and packaging materials are ready before orders come in.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the O in ROAST?
qa_pairs.output: O stands for Order Accuracy — auto-accept orders without cancellations, verify KOTs for order type/ID/special instructions/total items, and follow customer special instructions precisely.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the A in ROAST?
qa_pairs.output: A stands for Adherence to SOP — prepare every item per the standard operating procedure with no shortcuts, even during peak hours.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the S in ROAST?
qa_pairs.output: S stands for Sealing & Packaging — heat seal all cups, conduct spill checks, label cups with product name or special instructions, use tamper-proof stickers, and package in appropriate bags with holders.

qa_pairs.instruction: What is the T in ROAST?
qa_pairs.output: T stands for Timeliness — complete preparation and packaging quickly to meet delivery partner pickup times, ensuring freshness and customer satisfaction.

qa_pairs.instruction: How does ROAST relate to the HD touchpoints?
qa_pairs.output: ROAST maps to the 4 HD touchpoints: Readiness = Touchpoint 1 (portal activation), Order Accuracy = Touchpoint 2 (order receiving), Adherence to SOP = Touchpoint 3 (order preparation), Sealing & Packaging + Timeliness = Touchpoint 4 (packaging and handoff).
